coredata.dc:title: Experimental data-driven efficient exploration of the composition and process conditions of Li-rich NASICON-type solid electrolytes

Murakami, Kento

Nakayama, Masanobu

Ono, Yasuharu
Saito, Naohiko
coredata.dc:description: LiZr2(PO4)3 has garnered widespread interest as a solid electrolyte for all-solid-state batteries. However, its Li ionic conductivity remains insufficient for practical use. Although attempts have been made to improve the Li ionic conductivity by doping with cations and controlling the synthesis conditions, the exploration space is vast, and optimisation remains challenging. In this study, the amount of dopants and heating conditions for Li1+x+2yCayZr2-ySixP3-xO12 co-doped with Ca2+ and Si4+ were optimised via experimental synthesis, evaluation, and Bayesian optimisation (BO) cycles. The BO technique suggests the next experimental samples in each cycle and reduces the number of experimental cycles by almost 80 % compared with an exhaustive search. In addition, the experimental results were subjected to machine-learning regression analysis to analyse the factors affecting the Li-ion conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li ionic conductivity
Solid electrolyte
Bayesian optimisation
Machine-learning regression

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication conditions and conductivities selected by bayesian optimisation
Crystal phase identification by XRD measurement

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (Grant numbers 21K14715 24H02203, 24K01157, and 22H02179) from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology (MEXT), Japan; Data Creation and Utilisation-Type Material Research and Development Project (Grant number JPMXP1122712807) from MEXT; and JST CREST (Grant number JPMJCR21O6). We also thank Mr. Kanato Oka and Mr. Koichi Gocho for their assistance in data analysis. English language editing was performed by Editage (www.editage.com).

This work was partially supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (Grant numbers 19H05815, 20H02436, 21K04650, 21K14715, 21H01625, and 22H02179) from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology (MEXT), Japan; Data Creation and Utilisation-Type Material Research and Development Project (Grant number JPMXP1122712807) from MEXT; and JST CREST (Grant number JPMJCR21D3). We also thank Mr. Kanato Oka and Mr. Koichi Gocho for their assistance in data analysis. English language editing was performed by Editage ( www.editage.com ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Li-ion conductivities and dispersions from Gaussian process-based predictions, and experimental Li-ion conductivities.

XRD patterns of the samples synthesised at different heating conditions and ICSD simulated patterns of ɑ’-LiZr2(PO4)3(#89456), ɑ-LiZr2(PO4)3 (#201935), β-LiZr2(PO4)3 (#91113), and ZrSiO4 (#100243). (a) After first heating of the sample with (x,y) = (0.1, 0.1), (b) after second heating of the sample with (x,y) = (0.1, 0.1), and (c) after second heating of the sample with (x,y) = (0.4, 0.05).

Microstructures of the samples with (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) under different heating conditions (T1, T2) (a) (900, 1200), (b) (1000, 1200), (c) (1200, 1200), (d) (1000, 1250), (e) (1100, 1250), and (f) (1200, 1250). The ionic conductivity values shown in the figure are evaluated at 30 °C through AC impedance measurements, based on the sum of the bulk and grain boundary resistances (total ionic conductivity). The values are cited from Table S1.

(a) Bar graph of the RMSE in the test data using eight different machine-learning algorithms for Li-ion conductivity. (b) Diagnostic plots of the random forest regression analysis for Li-ion conductivity.
Li-ion conductivities predicted from random forest regression. (a) (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1); (b) (x, y) = (0.4, 0.05).
SHAP values for the top 20 most important descriptors for predicting Li-ion conductivities using random forest regression.
Search point conditions in this study.

head.ce:title: Experimental data-driven efficient exploration of the composition and process conditions of Li-rich NASICON-type solid electrolytes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Ceramics, Nagoya Institute of Technology, Gokiso, Showa, Nagoya, Aichi 466-8555, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Ceramics, Nagoya Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Advanced Ceramics, Nagoya Institute of Technology, Gokiso, Showa, Nagoya, Aichi 466-8555, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: TOAGOSEI CO., LTD., General Center of R&D, 8, Showa-cho, Minato-ku, Nagoya 455-0026, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: TOAGOSEI CO., LTD., General Center of R&D

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: TOAGOSEI CO., LTD., General Center of R&D, 8, Showa-cho, Minato-ku, Nagoya, 455-0026, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: LiZr2(PO4)3 has garnered widespread interest as a solid electrolyte for all-solid-state batteries. However, its Li ionic conductivity remains insufficient for practical use. Although attempts have been made to improve the Li ionic conductivity by doping with cations and controlling the synthesis conditions, the exploration space is vast, and optimisation remains challenging. In this study, the amount of dopants and heating conditions for Li1+x+2yCayZr2-ySixP3-xO12 co-doped with Ca2+ and Si4+ were optimised via experimental synthesis, evaluation, and Bayesian optimisation (BO) cycles. The BO technique suggests the next experimental samples in each cycle and reduces the number of experimental cycles by almost 80 % compared with an exhaustive search. In addition, the experimental results were subjected to machine-learning regression analysis to analyse the factors affecting the Li-ion conductivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Scripts, trained data and packages for Bayesian optimisation and machine-learning analysis and Supplementary Data 1 and 2 are available in Figshare: doi: 10.6084/m9.figshare.27239148 The authors declare that the data supporting the findings of this study are available in the paper and its supplementary material file.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, electric vehicles have been rapidly developed for reducing CO2 emissions and protecting the environment towards realising a decarbonised society [1]. Further, smart grids, which are next-generation energy systems that combine electric power networks with information and communication technologies, have been actively introduced to solve energy and environmental problems that have become more prominent in the 21st century [2]. Such applications require a significantly larger capacity and higher rate of operation compared to that of conventional lithium-ion rechargeable batteries, as well as improved safety. Conventional lithium-ion batteries use flammable and volatile organic solvents [3,4], which make the batteries prone to fires and explosions. Therefore, entirely solid-state batteries in which non-flammable inorganic solid electrolytes are used instead of organic solvents have been actively researched [5]. Inorganic solid electrolytes, which include sulfide [6–8], chloride [9–11], and oxide types [12–17] are referred to as inorganic solid electrolytes. Oxide-type inorganic solid electrolytes are chemically stable and safe; however, they exhibit low Li-ion conductivity. Therefore, NASICON-type solid electrolytes LiZr2(PO4)3 (hereafter referred to as LZP) with relatively high lithium-ion conductivity have attracted attention [18,19]. LZP has four crystal phases (α, α′, β, and β′) depending on the heating temperature. The α phase corresponds to a NASICON-type structure and has the highest conductivity [20,21]. LZP has been studied for Li-rich NASICON-type solid electrolytes with small amounts of single dopants such as Ca2+, Al3+, or Y3+ substituted at the Zr4+ site; the ionic conductivity of such substituted LZP surpasses that of unsubstituted LZP; in addition, optimal values for the amount of substitution have been reported [22–24]. The substitution of multiple dopants in LZP improved lithium-ion conductivity compared to that using only a single dopant [25–27]. Ionic conductivity shows complex variation with the type and quantity of the dopant. Additionally, LZP synthesis heating conditions influence the crystalline phase, density, microstructure, interparticle necking, and elemental distribution, leading to differences in lithium-ion conductivity [25,26,28]. Lithium-ion conductivity varies by several orders of magnitude, and further improvement in ion conductivity is expected by selecting the optimal heating conditions that consider the effects of dopants.

In this study, ionic conductivity data for Li1+x+2yCayZr2-ySixP3-xO12 (hereafter referred to as LCZSP) collected in our previous studies [26,28] were used for determining the optimal solution in a further extended system, that is, the material with the highest ionic conductivity. We previously performed separate studies on LCZSP, wherein we exhaustively measured and optimised the ionic conductivities by varying the substitution amounts of Ca and P in 49 different ways under fixed heating conditions [26]. In another study, we optimised 54 different heating conditions with a fixed composition [28]. In this study, we attempt to optimise the material by varying both the composition and heating conditions within an exploration space of 1152 combinations. The results of the previous study confirmed that increasing the amount of Ca reached the limit of the solid solution and generated impurities at the grain boundaries [26] Further, the formation temperature of the α-phase changed with an increase in the amount of substituted Si [26] The density was reduced by the evaporation of Li2O and P2O5 through heating at temperatures above 1350 °C [28]. Thus, changes in the structures and properties of LCZSP compounds are complicated, preventing traditional optimisation based on researchers’ intuition.

Materials informatics, which integrates materials and data sciences, has attracted attention as a tool for accelerating research and development in the field of materials [29–32]. Bayesian optimisation (BO) is one method which determines the next search point by focussing on the predicted value and variance of the search point [33]. In detail, the BO approach utilises a probabilistic model, such as a Gaussian process, based on observed data to utilise information across the entire search space. To determine the next evaluation point, an acquisition function selects the most promising candidate based on existing information. Compared to random sampling or grid search, BO is more efficient in exploration owing to its ability to reduce the required number of evaluations. It is particularly useful applications involving high evaluation costs, such as hyperparameter tuning of machine-learning models or searching for optimal compositions and/or process parameters in material design. We confirmed the improved efficiency of BO using exhaustively evaluated Li ionic conductivities reported in previous studies [26,28]. Therefore, we followed the suggestions for the composition and synthesis conditions of BO in this study and demonstrated the optimisation process. The BO process is a black box function [34] and therefore, we analysed the factors affecting Li-ion conductivity using machine-learning regression analysis.
The flowchart of this study is presented in Fig. 1.

Composition (doping amount of Si and Ca) and synthesis conditions are determined by BO based on Gaussian process regression, which quantifies the priority of unmeasured materials as an acquisition function and provides a ranking of the materials to be measured next. An expected improvement strategy that balances ‘exploration’ and ‘exploitation’ is selected to obtain the acquisition function. Exploitation prioritises choosing the best sample from the predicted values, and exploration chooses the most uncertain points in the database. We set four BO descriptors: the amounts of Si and Ca substitution (x, y), and the first and second heating temperatures (T1, T2). The experiments for sample preparation, characterisation, and ionic conductivity measurements were performed by referring to the ranking table output by BO. The 576 search point conditions targeted in this study are listed in Table 1. Furthermore, to improve the efficiency of the experimental procedure, three samples with equal heating conditions (first and second heating temperatures) were preferentially selected for simultaneous experimental verification because they can be heated simultaneously in a muffle furnace used in the experiments. BO enables us to rank all candidate materials (unmeasured materials); however, the top-ranking positions often cluster with similar compositions and/or synthesis conditions. The three samples were evaluated simultaneously, and therefore, we ranked the candidates as follows:
(1)
The sample with the highest acquisition function in BO is selected as the rank-1 sample.
(2)
The ionic conductivity of the rank-1 sample predicted using Gaussian process regression was temporarily added to the measured dataset as training data, and BO was performed again. The sample with the highest acquisition function is selected as the rank-2 sample.
(3)
The above procedure is repeated to re-rank all candidates, and subsequently, three samples are selected from the ranking table.
(4)
The measured dataset (training dataset) is updated using the measured values after the experimental procedure for the three selected samples.

In the first iteration of this study, the 102 data on Li-ion conductivities at 30 °C of LCZSP obtained in the previous study [26,28] were used as training data for BO. For samples that could not be measured due to insufficient sintering, a conductivity value of 1 × 10⁻⁸ S/cm, which is lower than the lowest measured value (2.61 ×10⁻⁸ S/cm), was assigned. All search points listed in Table 1 were ranked as described above.

The ranking points in the first iteration obtained in this manner are indicated by the blue box in Fig. 1. The top three points with equal heating conditions (first and second heating temperatures), marked with star symbols, were synthesised and characterised. In the second iteration, the training datasets were updated by adding the experimental Li-ion conductivity data of the three samples obtained in the first iteration, and the search points were ranked again using the BO. This was repeated until no improvement in the conductivity was observed.

Li2CO3 (Kishida Chemical, 99.5 %), CaCO3 (Kishida Chemical, 99.5 %), ZrO2 (Kishida Chemical, 99.9 %), SiO2 (Kojundo Chemical Laboratory, 99.9 %), and Zr(HPO4)∙1.5 H2O (TOAGOSEI) were used as raw materials. In this study, various compositions were synthesised using raw materials from the same batch to maintain consistency. They were weighed to obtain the composition selected by BO, and pellet samples were fabricated in the same way as in the previous study [28]. An automatic mixer was used to minimise operator’s dependency in the powder mixing process. The pellets were first heated (pre-fired) in air at the temperature selected by BO for 4 h, crushed, and pressed again using a hydraulic press at 130 MPa to form pellets (diameter = 10 mm and thickness = ∼1.5 mm). The second heating process (sintering) was conducted in air for 4 h at the temperature selected by the BO.

The ionic conductivity of the pellets after the second heating was determined from alternating current (AC) impedance measurements. The electrodes were prepared by polishing both surfaces with a 2000-grit abrasive paper and sputtering Au. The complex impedance was measured at 30, 60, and 90 °C using an impedance analyser (VMP-300, BioLogic) with an amplitude of 1 V and frequency range of 1–106 Hz. Separating the bulk and grain boundary resistance of LZP materials is difficult [26,28], and therefore, the total resistance R (including the bulk and grain boundary resistances) was used to calculate the total ionic conductivity σ (S·cm–1) using
(1)
σ =
L
R × S
where R , S , and L represent the resistance (Ω), sample area (cm2), and thickness of the sample (cm), respectively.

The crystalline phase of the samples was identified using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation on a MiniFlex 600 diffractometer (Rigaku). XRD measurements were performed on the surface of the polished bulk samples to efficiently determine the rough trends of the constituent phases. The micromorphologies of the samples were assessed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6360LV, JEOL).

A total of 138 Li-ion conductivity data at 30 °C obtained from previous studies [26,28] and this study were used as the training data. The explanatory variables (descriptors) included the composition ratio of the constituent elements, heating temperature, and their multipliers (33 in total). The objective variable was the logarithm of ionic conductivity. Regression analysis was performed using eight machine-learning algorithms: random forest regression[35] light gradient boosting machine (light GBM) [36] linear support vector regression (linear SVR) [37] kernel SVR [38] and ridge [39], lasso[40] partial least squares (PLS) [41] and elastic net regression [42]

The training and testing data sets comprise 80 and 20 %, respectively. Hyperparameters for the training dataset are obtained using the 10-fold cross-validation method. For the test dataset, the root mean square error (RMSE) [43], which is calculated from the predictions and experimental values, is used to select the best machine-learning algorithm.

Furthermore, an importance analysis of the descriptors is performed using Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) values from the predictions of the optimal model on the test data. In this study, the treeSHAP method recommended by Lundberg et al. [44] was used to gain insight into the importance of all variables and their characteristic effects on predictions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication conditions and conductivities selected by bayesian optimisation
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 lists the composition/heating conditions selected for each iteration, and Table S1 lists the results of conductivity measurements and activation energies. Although 36 compositions and 16 heating conditions (total 576 conditions) were set in the search space, 11 compositions and 10 heating statitioconditions (total 12 batches / 36 samples) were investigated for the BO and experimental evaluations. Fig. 2 shows the variation in the predicted values (yellow: error bars represent the variance of the predictions) and the experimental values (blue) with BO and experimental evaluation cycles. The No. 2 sample was brittle and conductivity measurements were not possible, and therefore, we used the predicted ionic conductivity as the experimental value. In the 13th iteration, no predicted value greater than the highest conductivity was obtained even after considering variance (as shown in Supplementary Data; all the predicted values are listed in Supplementary Data 1). Therefore, BO was terminated after 12 iterations (36 samples synthesised). As indicated in Table 2, in the first three iterations, composition (x, y) = (0.4, 0.05) and the lower and upper temperature limits of the heating conditions were selected that are far from the initial data set. Therefore, ‘exploration’ was prioritised because of their high predicted variance values. In the third iteration, the composition (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1), which is close to (x, y) = (0.15, 0.15) (the most abundant composition in the initial data set), is also included. This indicates that ‘exploitation’ began. From the fourth iteration onwards, the compositions (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) (0.15, 0.1), and (0.1, 0.15) were frequently selected, and the selection of the optimal condition proceeded with an emphasis on ‘exploitation’ rather than ‘exploration’; the dispersion decreased as iterations progressed (Fig. 2). However, in the seventh iteration, the predicted variance was larger than that in the sixth iteration presumably because compositions that were not selected by the sixth iteration were selected, and the ‘exploration’ tendency temporarily became stronger.

In this study, the material with the highest total ionic conductivity (3.1 × 10−5 S·cm–1) was found in the fourth iteration (Sample No. 10). The composition of this material was Li1.3Ca0.1Zr1.9Si0.1P2.9O12 and the first and second heating were performed at 1200 °C. The total number of search points in the previous study and this study was 635, and the total number of samples synthesised in the previous study and this study was 138. Therefore, the usefulness of the search by BO in this study was calculated using
(2)
635 − 138
635
× 100 ≈ 78.3 %

This formula resulted in an experimental efficiency improvement of ∼78 % compared to that with an exhaustive search. For an accurate efficiency evaluation, experiments should be conducted on all cases, and simulations that repeatedly apply BO should be performed. However, evaluating all 576 combinations is impractical. Before conducting the current exploration, we performed preliminary investigations on NASICON materials using sample spaces of approximately 50 [25,26,28] and 250 cases [31], achieving ∼70 % efficiency improvement in our previous study. Therefore, the 80 % efficiency observed in this study, which involved a larger sample space, could be considered reasonable.

Furthermore, statistical analysis was performed on the obtained experimental results and descriptors (molar amounts of constituent elements (Si, P, Ca, Zr, Li, and Si), and first and second heating temperatures) using ANOVA. Table S3 shows that all the p-values, calculated using ANOVA, are below 0.05, indicating that the four descriptors are significantly related to the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal phase identification by XRD measurement
ce:section.ce:para: For all prepared samples, crystal phase identification was performed using XRD measurements. Peaks corresponding to the α (ICSD # 201935), α’ (ICSD # 89456), or β phase (ICSD # 91113) of LiZr2(PO4)3 reported in the inorganic crystal structure database (ICSD) were identified. Impurity phases such as ZrO2, CaCO3, and ZrSiO4 were also detected. Fig. 3(a) shows the XRD patterns of the samples after first heating with (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1); these samples achieved the highest conductivity. Fig. 3(b) and (c) show the XRD patterns of the samples (prepared under different heating conditions) after second heating, with (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) and (0.4, 0.05), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the β phase preferentially forms at low first-heating temperatures, whereas the α phase formation dominates as the temperature increases. Despite the dependence of the constitutive phase, after the first heating, on the heating temperature, the α phase was the predominant phase in all the heating conditions (Fig. 3(b)), and no difference was observed between the heating conditions. The appearance of few diffraction peaks that can be attributed to the α' phase suggest that Ca doping lowered the transition temperature below room temperature [22]. In the Si-rich composition shown in Fig. 3(c), the β phase is the main phase; however, the peak of ZrSiO4 (ICSD# 100243) was also observed. At the second heating temperature of 1350 °C, the peaks originating from the β phase became smaller and the characteristic peaks of the α phase (red solid circle) became larger. This can be attributed to the increase in the Si weight fraction, which stabilises the β phase, increasing the phase transition temperature to the α phase [26].

Fig. 4 shows the microstructures of the surfaces of the samples with composition (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) under various heating conditions. These samples have the same composition as those with the highest ionic conductivity. The ionic conductivities of the samples were determined by AC impedance measurements (details are provided in Section 2.3). While the XRD patterns of these samples were almost identical (Fig. 3(a)), their ionic conductivities were different. The morphologies and sizes of the particles and grain boundaries of these samples differed (Fig. 4). The particles cracked in samples with low conductivity, which were heated under conditions (T1, T2) = (1100, 1250), (1200,1250) (Fig. 4(e and f), whereas the samples heated under the condition of (T1, T2) = (1200, 1200) (Fig. 4(c)) showed good particle-to-particle contacts after necking and grain boundary growth, indicating the highest ionic conductivity.

The relationship between bulk density and conductivity was investigated for the sample with the highest conductivity, (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1). Samples with high bulk densities tended to exhibit high conductivities (Fig. S1). To examine the influence of heating conditions on density, thermogravimetric analysis was performed on powder samples after the first heating. The samples were subjected to conditions corresponding to (T1, T2) = (900, 1200), (1200, 1200), and (1200, 1250) via first heating at 900 °C or 1200 °C and were then held at 1200 °C for 1 h, or via first heating at 1200 °C and were subsequently held at 1250 °C for 1 h. In addition, their weight loss was analysed, and the smallest weight loss was observed under the condition of (T1, T2) = (1200, 1200) (Fig. S2). This result indicates that when the first heating temperature is low, significant component evaporation occurs during the second heating. When the second heating temperature exceeds 1250 °C, components such as Li and P also evaporate. The significant heating-temperature-dependent differences between the XRD patterns recorded after the first heating, combined with the minimal differences observed after the second heating, suggest that the ionic-conductivity differences are independent of the crystal structure; rather, these differences are sensitive to the microstructure. These results collectively indicate that the first and second heating temperatures at the optimal composition, (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1), affect the microstructure and bulk density, which influence the resulting conductivity.

Machine-learning regression was performed for the measured ionic conductivity using descriptors derived from the composition and heating conditions. Fig. 5(a) shows the RMSE for each machine-learning regression algorithm in the test data, and Fig. 5(b) shows the diagnostic plots for random forest, which had the lowest RMSE value. Despite the presence of some outliers, the determination coefficient (R2) was 0.79, and most points were located near the ideal line y = x. Random forest is a nonlinear model, and therefore, the ionic conductivity is not linearly related to the descriptors used in this study.

The random forest-derived prediction function was used for calculating the conductivity in an expanded search space with additional first heating temperature conditions (950, 1050, 1150, and 1250 °C). All the predicted values are listed in Supplementary Data 2. The maximum value was obtained for the sample with condition (x, y, T1, T2) = (0.15, 0.15, 1050, 1250). This compositional and heating condition is consistent with the condition that shows the actual maximum value in the search space: 3.3 × 10−5 S·cm–1 [28] confirming the certainty of the random forest prediction. Hence, the random forest regression results support the convergence of the BO search targeting the highest ionic conductivity.

Fig. 6 shows the variations in the predicted ionic conductivities with respect to the heating temperature for selected compositions. This includes sample compositions that exhibited the highest conductivity: (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) (Fig. 6(a)) and the composition commonly selected in the first three BO iterations: (x, y) = (0.05, 0.4) (Fig. 6(b)). The composition (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) showed relatively high ionic conductivity although an increase in the second heating temperature caused a slight decrease in ionic conductivity. This is attributed to the evaporation of Li upon heating at high temperatures. The ionic conductivity of composition (x, y) = (0.4, 0.05) was approximately one order of magnitude lower than that of composition (x, y) = (0.1, 0.1) under all heating conditions; however, the conductivity tended to increase with heating temperature. This can be attributed to the β phase with lower ionic conductivity being predominant in this composition, and the α phase with higher ionic conductivity being formed only at higher heating temperatures, as shown in Fig. 3(c).

Fig. S3 shows the heating-temperature dependence of the highest predicted Li-ion conductivities (coloured plot) among various compositions. The optimised conductivity is observed at intermediate temperatures. The first heating temperature affects the formation phase in the fabrication process (Section 3.2), whereas the second heating temperature affects the sintering density (Section 3.3). Fig. S4 shows the compositional dependence of the highest predicted Li-ion conductivities (coloured plot) among various heating conditions. The conductivity decreases drastically when the Si content exceeds 0.2. However, doping small amounts of Si and Ca, which increases the number of Li interstitials, is effective in enhancing the conductivity [31]. In the present BO (Table 1), the second heating temperature consistently remained within the 1200–1250 °C range, and the Si composition was always selected to be below 0.2 after the eighth BO iteration. This result suggests a correlation between the results of machine-learning regression analysis and candidate sample selection in BO.

Subsequently, an analysis of importance was conducted to investigate the contribution of descriptive factors to Li-ion conductivity prediction. The SHAP values measure the influence of variables on model predictions, and positive or negative values indicate a greater impact on target variables. Fig. 7 shows the SHAP values of 20 of the most important descriptors used to predict Li-ion conductivity with the random forest model, with red indicating positive correlations and blue indicating negative correlations for each descriptor. The line thickness represents the frequency distribution of the data. The descriptor of the highest importance in this study is Si × Li (multiplying the number of Si atoms by the number of Li atoms) The second most important descriptors were Zr × second heating (multiplying the number of Zr atoms by the second heating temperature). The SHAP results reconfirm that the top two factors, Si × Li and Zr × second heating, have the most significant influence. These factors, represented as the product of two variables, are not independent of the target variable. In other words, the effects of these combinations are interrelated. When the feature value of the most influential factor, Si × Li, becomes large, the SHAP value becomes negative, indicating a decrease in the value of the target variable. Conversely, when the feature value is minimum, the SHAP value is negative, suggesting a trade-off; this trade-off may be attributed to the conductivity enhancement due to Ca or Si doping-induced increase in Li concentration at interstitial sites owing to improves ionic conductivity; however, excessive Si doping ptomote β-phase formation. Similarly, a trade-off is observed for the second factor Zr × sintering as the SHAP value becomes negative at the maximum or minimum feature values. The second heating temperature (sintering) promotes α-phase formation and increases the sintering density, thus reducing grain boundary resistance. However, it may also promote the evaporation of volatile components. [28]; this result suggests a correlation between Zr content and second-heating-temperature control. In contrast, many other highly important descriptors were combinations of two descriptors, suggesting that changes in a single composition or process parameters do not directly determine ion conductivity. Instead, multiple factors that can have positive or negative effects on ion conductivity are involved. This results in trade-offs, indicating that precise control of both the composition and process parameters is necessary for achieving the optimal values.

Experimental materials were searched for fast Li-ion conductors by applying BO on Li1+x+2yCayZr2-ySixP3-xO12 materials. The search space had 576 conditions considering composition x, y, and first and second heating temperatures, and the samples were investigated using 102 pre-investigated data points (a total of 138 samples were experimentally evaluated). The BO approach searched for high-conductivity Li1+x+2yCayZr2-ySixP3-xO12 materials by creating samples in ∼20 % of the search space. Furthermore, machine-learning regression analysis was performed, and the best-fitting result showed a coefficient of determination of ∼0.8. The results indicate that the second heating temperature and the amount of dopant are important variables for predicting ionic conductivities.

H. T., M. N., Y. O., and N. S. conceived and designed the study. K. M., H. F., and T. N. performed the experiments and analysed the results. K. M., and Y. Y. conducted the computational studies and analysed the data. The manuscript was written by H. T. and M. N., with contributions from all authors. All authors approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by a Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (Grant numbers 21K14715 24H02203, 24K01157, and 22H02179) from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology (MEXT), Japan; Data Creation and Utilisation-Type Material Research and Development Project (Grant number JPMXP1122712807) from MEXT; and JST CREST (Grant number JPMJCR21O6). We also thank Mr. Kanato Oka and Mr. Koichi Gocho for their assistance in data analysis. English language editing was performed by Editage (www.editage.com).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxmate.2025.100574.
